---
title: "Tutorial 3: Build a Domain Plugin"
subtitle: "Scaffold, one skill, one evidence-linked concept, one green golden notebook (~30 minutes)"
format: html
---

<!-- Surfaces verified: Claude Code 2026-07-05 (scaffold-to-installed,
     12.3 minutes measured). Building plugins is a Code-side activity;
     the plugin you build installs on all three surfaces. -->

**Surfaces verified:** Claude Code. Building is Code-side; what you
build installs everywhere.

## What you will do

Turn the plugin-template into a tiny working domain plugin: one
workflow skill with a gate, one knowledge concept with real evidence,
one golden notebook that runs green headless, installed and tested.
The point is the SHAPE: after this, scaling up is repetition.

## 1. Scaffold (3 minutes)

```bash
git clone https://github.com/open-science-pillars/plugin-template my-plugin
cd my-plugin && rm -rf .git && git init -b main
```

Edit `.claude-plugin/plugin.json`: your plugin's name, a keyword-first
description under 200 characters, your homepage. The layout you
inherited is the whole contract: `skills/`, `knowledge/`,
`verification/`, `evals/`, no `commands/` anywhere.

## 2. One skill (8 minutes)

Rename `skills/example-workflow` to something in your domain and
rewrite its SKILL.md keeping the shape: frontmatter (name +
description), a numbered behavior sequence with a
**confirmation gate before any side effect**, and Must NOT rules. The
[skill authoring guide](https://github.com/open-science-pillars/marketplace/blob/main/docs/skill-authoring-guide.md)
carries the patterns; the annotated example you are editing explains
each piece inline.

## 3. One knowledge concept with evidence (8 minutes)

Copy the matching example from the
[knowledge-template](https://github.com/open-science-pillars/knowledge-template)
(dataset, gotcha, recipe, or convention) into `knowledge/` and fill it
with something TRUE about your domain: a real trap, with a real link
that supports it. Rules that are enforced, not suggested: quote YAML
values containing colons; every gotcha claim carries an evidence link;
concepts state facts, never instructions to the agent; update
`knowledge/index.md` and `log.md`. Lint it by asking Claude (with the
core plugin installed) to run the knowledge-linter on your bundle.

## 4. One golden notebook (6 minutes)

Adapt `verification/example_workflow.py`: synthesize a small
deterministic fixture in-notebook, run your skill's computation,
assert the expected result AND its uncertainty range. Green means:

```bash
python verification/your_workflow.py; echo $?   # must print 0
```

A skill without a green golden is not done; that rule is what lets
later refactors run fast.

## 5. Install and test (5 minutes)

Push to a Git host, then:

```bash
claude plugin marketplace add <your-github-user>/my-plugin
claude plugin install my-plugin@<marketplace-name>
```

(or add the folder as a local marketplace). Open a fresh Claude Code
session: your skill appears in `/skills`, invokes by slash and
conversationally, and its gate fires before side effects. Ask a
question your concept covers and watch it get cited.

## The applied variant: teach it ARSET-style

To turn your plugin into a training, fill the
[arset-style template](https://github.com/open-science-pillars/tutorials/blob/main/templates/arset-style.md):
objectives, audience, prerequisites (link ARSET Fundamentals where
apt), 2-4 parts each with materials/steps/checkpoint, a hands-on
exercise whose expected range comes from a recipe concept, and a
completion checklist. That structure is how this project's own
tutorials are tested: measurable outcomes, timed, verified surfaces
recorded.

**Timing note:** scaffold-to-installed measured at 12.3 minutes on
2026-07-05, including linting the new bundle and negative-testing the
golden; well inside the 30-minute target. Two ecosystem wrinkles you
may hit: marketplace plugin sources must live at or below the
marketplace directory (use `./plugin-name`, symlinking if needed), and
`claude plugin update` is version-gated, so same-version refreshes need
uninstall/reinstall.
